 {"id":"bd-1ez8","title":"Set up automated nightly rebase CI for WezTerm fork","description":"## Overview

WezTerm is updated nightly by its author. To maintain our feature branch, we need automated rebasing with conflict detection and alerting.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: bd-20fw (WezTerm fork with wa integration)

## CI Workflow Design

### GitHub Actions Workflow

\`\`\`.github/workflows/rebase-upstream.yml\`\`\`
\`\`\`yaml
name: Rebase on Upstream WezTerm

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'  # Daily at 6 AM UTC
  workflow_dispatch:      # Manual trigger

jobs:
  rebase:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
          ref: feature/wa-integration
          token: \${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      
      - name: Configure Git
        run: |
          git config user.name "wa-bot"
          git config user.email "wa-bot@example.com"
      
      - name: Add Upstream Remote
        run: |
          git remote add upstream https://github.com/wez/wezterm.git
          git fetch upstream main
      
      - name: Attempt Rebase
        id: rebase
        continue-on-error: true
        run: |
          git rebase upstream/main
          echo "rebase_status=\$?" >> \$GITHUB_OUTPUT
      
      - name: Push if Successful
        if: steps.rebase.outcome == 'success'
        run: |
          git push --force-with-lease origin feature/wa-integration
      
      - name: Create Conflict Issue
        if: steps.rebase.outcome == 'failure'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const { data: issues } = await github.rest.issues.listForRepo({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              labels: 'rebase-conflict',
              state: 'open'
            });
            
            if (issues.length === 0) {
              await github.rest.issues.create({
                owner: context.repo.owner,
                repo: context.repo.repo,
                title: 'Rebase conflict with upstream WezTerm',
                body: \`The nightly rebase on upstream WezTerm failed due to conflicts.
                
                **Action Required**: Manual resolution needed.
                
                \\\`\\\`\\\`bash
                git fetch upstream main
                git rebase upstream/main
                # Resolve conflicts
                git rebase --continue
                git push --force-with-lease
                \\\`\\\`\\\`
                \`,
                labels: ['rebase-conflict', 'urgent']
              });
            }
      
      - name: Run Tests After Rebase
        if: steps.rebase.outcome == 'success'
        run: |
          cargo build --features wa-integration
          cargo test --features wa-integration

  notify:
    needs: rebase
    if: failure()
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Send Notification
        run: |
          # Notify via email, Slack, Discord, etc.
          echo "Rebase failed - notification would be sent"
\`\`\`

### Handling Conflicts

When conflicts occur:
1. CI creates GitHub issue with "rebase-conflict" label
2. Human/agent reviews conflicting files
3. Usually conflicts are in files we modified (minimal set)
4. Resolve, test, push

### Minimizing Conflicts

To reduce conflict frequency:
1. Keep wa changes behind \`#[cfg(feature)]\` — no logic changes to upstream code
2. Use separate files where possible (mux/src/wa_events.rs)
3. Add wa code at end of functions, not middle
4. Avoid reformatting upstream code

## Alternative: Patch-Based Approach

Instead of maintaining a branch, maintain a patch series:

\`\`\`bash
# Generate patches
git format-patch upstream/main..feature/wa-integration -o patches/

# Apply patches to fresh clone
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/wez/wezterm.git
cd wezterm
git am ../patches/*.patch
\`\`\`

Patches are more explicit about what we change, easier to review.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] GitHub Actions workflow created
- [ ] Nightly rebase runs automatically
- [ ] Conflicts create GitHub issues
- [ ] Notifications configured
- [ ] Post-rebase tests run
- [ ] Documentation for manual conflict resolution
- [ ] 30-day trial period: track conflict frequency

## Files to Create

- .github/workflows/rebase-upstream.yml
- docs/maintaining-wezterm-fork.md

## References

- GitHub Actions: https://docs.github.com/en/actions
- Git rebase: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-rebase
- WezTerm release cadence: nightly builds","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:53.398103664Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T21:50:03.655977304Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ez8","depends_on_id":"bd-20fw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:50:03.655935456Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ez8","depends_on_id":"bd-2xe4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:53.407112375Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1zgb","title":"E2E: reliability hardening scenarios with verbose logs","description":"## Goal
Validate resilience features end-to-end with detailed logging and artifacts.

## Requirements
- Execute fault injection scenarios that trigger circuit breaker, retry, and graceful degradation paths.
- Capture verbose logs (trace/debug) plus key metrics snapshots as artifacts.
- Verify user-facing output is stable and actionable during degraded mode.

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E scenarios pass locally and in CI.
- Failure artifacts include full logs, triggered fault labels, and observed policy outputs.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T20:20:24.619323361Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-19T20:43:47.826611461Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1zgb","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:58Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1zgb","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:58Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-1zgb","depends_on_id":"wa-37x","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:09:58Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-20fw","title":"Create WezTerm fork with wa feature flag and minimal integration points","description":"## Overview

Fork WezTerm and add the minimal code needed to support wa event delivery. All changes are behind \`#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]\`.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: bd-3gon (WaEventSink trait design)

## Repository Setup

### 1. Fork WezTerm
\`\`\`bash
gh repo fork wez/wezterm --clone
cd wezterm
git checkout -b feature/wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Add Feature Flag
In Cargo.toml (workspace root):
\`\`\`toml
[workspace.features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

In relevant crates (mux/Cargo.toml, wezterm/Cargo.toml):
\`\`\`toml
[features]
wa-integration = []
\`\`\`

## Modification Points

### 1. mux/src/lib.rs — Event Sink Registration

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
mod wa_events;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
use wa_events::WaEventSink;

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
static WA_EVENT_SINK: OnceCell<Arc<dyn WaEventSink>> = OnceCell::new();

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub fn register_wa_event_sink(sink: Arc<dyn WaEventSink>) {
    WA_EVENT_SINK.set(sink).ok();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
pub(crate) fn emit_wa_event(event: impl FnOnce(&dyn WaEventSink)) {
    if let Some(sink) = WA_EVENT_SINK.get() {
        event(sink.as_ref());
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit Output Events

In the method that receives PTY output:
\`\`\`rust
fn process_pty_output(&mut self, data: &[u8]) {
    // Existing WezTerm code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_output(self.pane_id, data);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 3. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit State Changes

In methods that update pane state:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_title(&mut self, title: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    self.emit_state_change();
}

#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn emit_state_change(&self) {
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_pane_state_change(self.pane_id, &self.get_wa_state());
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 4. mux/src/pane.rs — Emit User-Var Changes

In the OSC 1337 handler:
\`\`\`rust
fn set_user_var(&mut self, name: String, value: String) {
    // Existing code...
    
    #[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
    crate::emit_wa_event(|sink| {
        sink.on_user_var_changed(self.pane_id, &name, &value);
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 5. wezterm/src/main.rs — Initialize Sink

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "wa-integration")]
fn init_wa_integration() {
    if let Ok(socket_path) = std::env::var("WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET") {
        match wa_socket_sink::connect(&socket_path) {
            Ok(sink) => mux::register_wa_event_sink(sink),
            Err(e) => log::warn!("Failed to connect to wa socket: {}", e),
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing the Fork

### 1. Build with Feature
\`\`\`bash
cargo build --features wa-integration
\`\`\`

### 2. Verify Default Build Unaffected
\`\`\`bash
cargo build  # Should work identically to upstream
\`\`\`

### 3. Integration Test
\`\`\`bash
# Start wa watch (which creates the socket)
wa watch &

# Start WezTerm with wa integration
WEZTERM_WA_SOCKET=/tmp/wa/events.sock ./target/debug/wezterm

# Verify events received
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] WezTerm fork created with feature/wa-integration branch
- [ ] Feature flag added to workspace
- [ ] Event emission added to pane output path
- [ ] Event emission added to state change paths
- [ ] Event emission added to user-var handler
- [ ] Default build (no feature) compiles and runs unchanged
- [ ] Feature build compiles and runs
- [ ] Events actually received by wa (integration test)

## Risks

- WezTerm internal APIs may change — minimize touchpoints
- Performance regression — benchmark before/after
- Thread safety bugs — careful with event emission

## Files to Modify (in WezTerm fork)

- Cargo.toml (workspace) — add feature
- mux/Cargo.toml — add feature
- mux/src/lib.rs — event sink registration
- mux/src/pane.rs — event emission
- wezterm/Cargo.toml — add feature
- wezterm/src/main.rs — initialization
- NEW: mux/src/wa_events.rs — trait + socket sink implementation

## References

- WezTerm mux: https://github.com/wez/wezterm/tree/main/mux
- Rust feature flags: https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/features.html","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:14.103113431Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:24.240940549Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-20fw","depends_on_id":"bd-2xe4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:14.123064266Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-20fw","depends_on_id":"bd-3gon","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:49:24.240909832Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"bd-21ml","title":"Benchmark WezTerm performance before/after STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removal","description":"## Overview

Measure WezTerm responsiveness before and after removing STATUS_UPDATE_LUA to quantify the performance improvement. This validates the optimization and provides data for the decision to pursue (or skip) Phase 2/3.

## Methodology

### 1. Baseline Measurement (Before)

With STATUS_UPDATE_LUA active:

\`\`\`bash
# Terminal responsiveness test
time for i in {1..1000}; do echo "line $i"; done

# Scrollback stress test
seq 1 10000 | while read n; do echo "Stress test line $n with some padding text to fill the line"; done

# Interactive typing latency (subjective)
# Type rapidly and observe lag
\`\`\`

### 2. Measurement (After)

Same tests with STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed.

### 3. Metrics to Capture

| Metric | How to Measure |
|--------|----------------|
| Echo throughput | Lines/second in echo loop |
| Scroll smoothness | Subjective + frame timing |
| Input latency | Time from keypress to echo (harder to measure) |
| CPU usage | \`htop\` or \`perf\` during tests |
| WezTerm frame rate | If profiling tools available |

### 4. Test Scenarios

1. **Idle pane**: Just cursor blinking — update-status fires constantly
2. **Active output**: \`yes\` or \`seq\` flooding terminal
3. **Multiple panes**: 4+ panes, some active
4. **With wa watch running**: Baseline vs optimized
5. **Without wa watch**: Pure WezTerm baseline

## Tools

### CPU Profiling
\`\`\`bash
# Linux perf
perf record -g -p $(pgrep wezterm) -- sleep 10
perf report

# macOS Instruments
instruments -t "Time Profiler" -p $(pgrep wezterm) -D profile.trace
\`\`\`

### Frame Timing
WezTerm may have debug options for frame timing. Check config:
\`\`\`lua
config.debug_key_events = true
-- Other debug options
\`\`\`

### Memory
\`\`\`bash
# Watch WezTerm memory usage
while true; do ps -o rss= -p $(pgrep wezterm); sleep 1; done
\`\`\`

## Expected Results

| Scenario | Before (Lua) | After (No Lua) | Improvement |
|----------|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| Idle CPU | ~5-15% | ~1-3% | 3-5x |
| Echo throughput | X lines/s | Y lines/s | TBD |
| Typing latency | Perceptible lag | No lag | Subjective |

## Recording Results

Document results in:
1. This bead's comments (use \`br comments add\`)
2. PR description when merging
3. CHANGELOG.md under "Performance" section

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Baseline measurements recorded (before)
- [ ] Post-optimization measurements recorded (after)
- [ ] Improvement quantified (percentage or ratio)
- [ ] Results documented
- [ ] User confirms subjective improvement
- [ ] Decision: proceed to Phase 2/3 or not?

## Dependencies

- Should be done: After bd-30gj (STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed)
- Before: bd-mwst (documentation) — to include benchmark results

## Files

- Create: benchmarks/lua_removal_results.md (or add to docs/)

## Notes

This is a **validation task**. If results don't show improvement, we need to investigate why (maybe the bottleneck is elsewhere).","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:05.693319255Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T22:53:12.445011242Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-21ml","depends_on_id":"bd-2l5s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:05.709998681Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-21ml","depends_on_id":"bd-30gj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:15.720326696Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

If results don't show improvement, we need to investigate why (maybe the bottleneck is elsewhere).","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:05.693319255Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T23:09:13.713378695Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T23:09:13.713234457Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-21ml","depends_on_id":"bd-2l5s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:05.709998681Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-21ml","depends_on_id":"bd-30gj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:52:15.720326696Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":76,"issue_id":"bd-21ml","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Benchmark Status

**Note**: Baseline measurements (before STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removal) were not captured before the optimization was implemented. The code was removed as part of bd-30gj and bd-2z6d.

### Theoretical Improvement

The `update-status` Lua event was firing at ~60Hz (every frame), which caused:
- Continuous Lua interpreter invocations
- Repeated JSON serialization of pane state
- IPC overhead for each status update

With STATUS_UPDATE_LUA removed:
- Alt-screen detection now uses escape sequence parsing (zero Lua overhead)
- Pane metadata obtained from `wezterm cli list` only when needed
- No continuous polling or event firing

### Recommendation

Close this task and document the change in bd-mwst. User can validate subjective improvement by testing WezTerm responsiveness with/without `wa watch` running.

### Files Changed (for reference)
- Removed STATUS_UPDATE_LUA from setup.rs
- Removed StatusUpdate types/handlers from ipc.rs  
- Removed StatusUpdateReceived event from events.rs
- Created screen_state.rs for escape sequence-based alt-screen detection

 {"id":"bd-2kl3","title":"[EPIC] Phase 3: Optimize Remaining Lua (user-var hook) — If Still Needed","description":"## Overview

After Phase 1 (remove STATUS_UPDATE_LUA), the only remaining Lua code is the user-var forwarding hook. This epic covers potential optimizations if that hook still causes performance issues.

**Priority P3**: This is a contingency. The user-var hook triggers infrequently (only when agents emit signals), so it's unlikely to be a bottleneck. Only pursue if performance testing shows issues.

## Remaining Lua Code

After Phase 1, wezterm.lua will contain only:

\`\`\`lua
-- Forward user-var events to wa daemon
wezterm.on('user-var-changed', function(window, pane, name, value)
  if name:match('^wa%-') then
    wezterm.background_child_process {
      'wa', 'event', '--from-uservar',
      '--pane', tostring(pane:pane_id()),
      '--name', name,
      '--value', value
    }
  end
end)
\`\`\`

## Why This Is Likely Fine

1. **Low frequency**: user-var-changed only fires when agents emit OSC 1337 sequences
2. **Filtered**: Only wa-prefixed vars trigger the handler
3. **Efficient check**: String prefix match is fast in Lua
4. **Non-blocking**: wezterm.background_child_process doesn't block the UI

## Potential Optimizations (If Needed)

### Option A: Remove Name Prefix Check in Lua

Move filtering to wa CLI:

**Before (filter in Lua)**:
\`\`\`lua
if name:match('^wa%-') then
  -- spawn process
end
\`\`\`

**After (filter in wa)**:
\`\`\`lua
wezterm.background_child_process {
  'wa', 'event', '--from-uservar',
  '--pane', tostring(pane:pane_id()),
  '--name', name,
  '--value', value
}
-- wa CLI ignores non-wa-prefixed vars
\`\`\`

**Trade-off**: More process spawns, but simpler Lua.

### Option B: Batching (Complex)

If many user-vars are emitted rapidly:
- Collect in Lua table
- Emit batch every N ms
- Reduces process spawns

**Trade-off**: Added complexity, latency.

### Option C: Replace with Native (Phase 2)

If using vendored WezTerm (Phase 2), user-var events go through native path anyway. No Lua needed.

## When to Pursue This Epic

1. Complete Phase 1
2. Benchmark: Is WezTerm still slow with just user-var hook?
3. Profile: Is user-var hook actually the bottleneck?
4. If yes to both: implement optimizations

## Success Criteria

- User-var forwarding works with acceptable performance
- No perceptible WezTerm slowdown during agent operation
- Latency for user-var events <100ms

## References

- USERVAR_FORWARDING_LUA: crates/wa-core/src/setup.rs lines 33-47

 {"id":"wa-2tk","title":"Metrics export: Prometheus endpoint with counters, gauges, histograms","description":"# Metrics Export

## Purpose
Expose Prometheus-compatible metrics for monitoring and alerting.

## Implementation

### Metrics Registry
```rust
use prometheus::{Registry, Counter, CounterVec, Gauge, GaugeVec, Histogram, HistogramVec};

lazy_static\! {
    pub static ref REGISTRY: Registry = Registry::new();
    
    // Counters
    pub static ref EVENTS_DETECTED: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_events_detected_total",
        "Total events detected",
        &["type", "agent"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref WORKFLOWS_COMPLETED: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_workflows_completed_total",
        "Total workflows completed",
        &["workflow", "status"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref POLICY_DECISIONS: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_policy_decisions_total",
        "Total policy decisions",
        &["decision", "action"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Gauges
    pub static ref PANES_OBSERVED: Gauge = register_gauge\!(
        "wa_panes_observed",
        "Number of observed panes"
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref QUEUE_DEPTH: Gauge = register_gauge\!(
        "wa_queue_depth",
        "Current event queue depth"
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Histograms
    pub static ref PATTERN_MATCH_DURATION: Histogram = register_histogram\!(
        "wa_pattern_match_duration_seconds",
        "Time to run pattern matching",
        vec\![0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1]
    ).unwrap();
}
```

### HTTP Endpoint
```rust
use axum::{Router, routing::get};
use prometheus::Encoder;

async fn metrics_handler() -> impl IntoResponse {
    let encoder = prometheus::TextEncoder::new();
    let metric_families = REGISTRY.gather();
    let mut buffer = Vec::new();
    encoder.encode(&metric_families, &mut buffer).unwrap();
    (
        [(header::CONTENT_TYPE, "text/plain; charset=utf-8")],
        buffer,
    )
}

pub fn metrics_router() -> Router {
    Router::new().route("/metrics", get(metrics_handler))
}
```

### Metric Instrumentation Points
- Event detection: increment counter, record duration
- Workflow execution: increment counter by status
- Policy decisions: increment by decision type
- Pane changes: update gauge
- Pattern matching: record histogram

## Metrics Catalog

| Metric | Type | Labels | Description |
|--------|------|--------|-------------|
| wa_events_detected_total | counter | type, agent | Events detected |
| wa_workflows_completed_total | counter | workflow, status | Workflow completions |
| wa_policy_decisions_total | counter | decision, action | Policy evaluations |
| wa_panes_observed | gauge | - | Active panes |
| wa_queue_depth | gauge | - | Pending events |
| wa_db_size_bytes | gauge | - | Database size |
| wa_pattern_match_duration_seconds | histogram | - | Pattern match time |
| wa_workflow_step_duration_seconds | histogram | workflow, step | Step execution time |

## Testing
- Unit: Metrics increment correctly
- Integration: Endpoint serves valid Prometheus format
- E2E: Metrics change during workflow execution

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All core operations instrumented
- [ ] /metrics endpoint serves Prometheus format
- [ ] Cardinality kept reasonable (bounded labels)
- [ ] Histograms have sensible buckets
- [ ] Feature-flagged for minimal deployments

 {"id":"wa-35l","title":"wa robot workflow list: discover available workflows","description":"# wa robot workflow list

## Purpose
Allow agents to discover what workflows are available and their requirements.

## Command Interface
```bash
# List all workflows
wa robot workflow list

# Filter by trigger type
wa robot workflow list --trigger=event

# Include detailed descriptions
wa robot workflow list --verbose

# Filter by enabled status
wa robot workflow list --enabled

# JSON output (default in robot mode)
wa robot workflow list --json
```

## JSON Output Schema
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "workflow.list",
  "data": {
    "workflows": [
      {
        "name": "handle_compaction",
        "description": "Re-inject context after agent compaction",
        "enabled": true,
        "trigger_event_types": ["session.compaction"],
        "trigger_rule_ids": ["compaction.detected"],
        "agent_types": ["codex", "claude_code", "gemini"],
        "step_count": 5,
        "parameters": [],
        "requires_pane": true,
        "requires_approval": false,
        "can_abort": true,
        "destructive": false,
        "dependencies": []
      }
    ],
    "total": 5,
    "enabled_count": 4
  }
}
```

## Use Cases
1. **Discovery**: New agent learns what automations are available
2. **Capability check**: Agent verifies workflow exists before calling
3. **Documentation**: Generate help text from workflow metadata
4. **Debugging**: See what workflows could handle an event

## Implementation Notes
- Load workflow definitions from WorkflowRegistry
- Include both built-in and custom workflows
- Mark workflows with missing dependencies
- Filter by trigger type, enabled status

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_list_all_workflows() {
    let registry = setup_test_registry();
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list"]);
    
    assert!(output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    let workflows = output["data"]["workflows"].as_array().unwrap();
    assert!(workflows.len() > 0);
    
    // Verify required fields present
    let wf = &workflows[0];
    assert!(wf["name"].is_string());
    assert!(wf["enabled"].is_boolean());
}

#[test]
fn test_list_filter_by_trigger() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list", "--trigger=event"]);
    
    let workflows = output["data"]["workflows"].as_array().unwrap();
    for wf in workflows {
        assert!(wf["trigger_event_types"].as_array().unwrap().len() > 0);
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_list_filter_enabled_only() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list", "--enabled"]);
    
    let workflows = output["data"]["workflows"].as_array().unwrap();
    for wf in workflows {
        assert!(wf["enabled"].as_bool().unwrap());
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_list_json_schema_validation() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list"]);
    validate_against_schema(&output, "wa-robot-workflow-list.json");
}

#[test]
fn test_list_verbose_includes_descriptions() {
    let output = execute_robot(&["workflow", "list", "--verbose"]);
    
    let workflows = output["data"]["workflows"].as_array().unwrap();
    for wf in workflows {
        assert!(wf["description"].is_string());
        assert!(!wf["description"].as_str().unwrap().is_empty());
    }
}
```

### E2E Test
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# e2e_workflow_list.sh
set -euo pipefail
LOG="${ARTIFACT_DIR:-/tmp}/workflow_list.log"
log() { echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] $*" | tee -a "$LOG"; }

log "=== Workflow List E2E ==="

# 1. Basic list
log "Testing basic list..."
LIST=$(wa robot workflow list)
log "Result: $LIST"
echo "$LIST" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: ok not true"; exit 1; }
echo "$LIST" | jq -e '.data.workflows | length > 0' || { log "FAIL: no workflows"; exit 1; }

# 2. Filter by trigger
log "Testing trigger filter..."
FILTERED=$(wa robot workflow list --trigger=event)
log "Filtered: $FILTERED"
echo "$FILTERED" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: filter failed"; exit 1; }

# 3. Verify counts
log "Verifying counts..."
TOTAL=$(echo "$LIST" | jq '.data.total')
ENABLED=$(echo "$LIST" | jq '.data.enabled_count')
log "Total: $TOTAL, Enabled: $ENABLED"
[ "$ENABLED" -le "$TOTAL" ] || { log "FAIL: enabled > total"; exit 1; }

log "=== PASS: workflow_list ==="
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Lists all registered workflows
- [ ] Includes trigger and agent type info
- [ ] Shows dependency requirements
- [ ] --trigger filter works
- [ ] --enabled filter works
- [ ] --verbose includes full descriptions
- [ ] JSON validates against wa-robot-workflow-list.json schema
- [ ] Unit tests pass
- [ ] E2E test passes with detailed logging

 {"id":"wa-37x","title":"[EPIC] Reliability Hardening: Circuit Breakers, Chaos Testing, Graceful Degradation","description":"# [EPIC] Reliability Hardening

## Mission
Make wa **resilient to failures** at every level—external services, internal components, and edge cases.

## Why This Matters
wa controls terminal interactions. Failures must be handled gracefully:
- Browser automation fails → do not spam retries
- WezTerm CLI hangs → do not block forever
- DB corrupts → continue observing, degrade gracefully

Reliability is a trust multiplier. Users need confidence that wa will not make things worse when something goes wrong.

## Components

### 1. Circuit Breaker Pattern
Stop hammering failed services:
```
Service: browser_auth
Status: OPEN (tripped 3 failures)
Last failure: 2026-01-18T14:32:01 (timeout)
Retry after: 2026-01-18T14:37:01 (5 min cooldown)
```

Circuit breakers for:
- Browser automation (auth flows)
- `caut` CLI wrapper
- WezTerm CLI (if consistently failing)
- External webhooks

### 2. Watchdog/Heartbeat System
Monitor wa's own health:
- Heartbeat thread checks main loop liveness
- Deadlock detection (no progress for N seconds)
- Auto-restart on unrecoverable hang
- Crash report with diagnostic context

### 3. Chaos Testing Harness
Inject failures to prove resilience:
- Kill WezTerm mid-capture
- Corrupt DB file
- Simulate network timeouts
- Exhaust disk space
- Fill queues to capacity

Chaos tests validate:
- Data integrity after failures
- Graceful degradation behavior
- Recovery procedures work

### 4. Retry with Exponential Backoff
Standardized retry policy:
- Initial: 100ms
- Max: 30s
- Backoff factor: 2x
- Jitter: ±10%
- Max attempts: configurable

Applied to:
- WezTerm CLI calls
- DB writes (transient lock conflicts)
- External service calls

### 5. Graceful Degradation Modes
When components fail, continue with reduced functionality:
- DB read-only → continue observing, queue writes
- Pattern engine error → skip detection, keep ingesting
- Workflow failure → pause workflow, keep observing
- WezTerm CLI unavailable → retry with backoff, surface status

## Observability
All reliability mechanisms emit structured logs:
- Circuit state changes
- Retry attempts and outcomes
- Degradation mode transitions
- Watchdog warnings

## Testing
- Unit tests: Circuit breaker state machine
- Integration tests: Retry behavior under failure injection
- Chaos tests: System survives failure scenarios
- Load tests: Behavior under resource exhaustion

## Success Criteria
- Circuit breakers prevent retry storms
- Watchdog detects and recovers from hangs
- Chaos tests pass for critical failure scenarios
- Graceful degradation maintains core observability

## Acceptance Criteria
- Circuit breakers, retries, and watchdogs prevent runaway failures.
- Graceful degradation keeps core commands usable.
- Chaos harness runs without crashes and produces actionable logs.

-{"id":"wa-37x.1","title":"Circuit breaker infrastructure: state machine, cooldowns, and status reporting","description":"
# Circuit Breaker Infrastructure

## Purpose
Implement a generic circuit breaker pattern to prevent retry storms when services fail.

## State Machine
```
     failures < threshold
CLOSED ◄────────────────────────► OPEN
  │                                  │
  │ failure >= threshold             │ cooldown elapsed
  └──────────────────►               ▼
                              HALF-OPEN
                                  │
                    success → CLOSED
                    failure → OPEN
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct CircuitBreaker {
    name: String,
    state: CircuitState,
    failure_count: u32,
    failure_threshold: u32,
    cooldown: Duration,
    last_failure: Option<Instant>,
}

impl CircuitBreaker {
    pub fn call<T>(&self, f: impl FnOnce() -> Result<T>) -> Result<T, CircuitError>;
    pub fn record_success(&mut self);
    pub fn record_failure(&mut self, error: &Error);
    pub fn status(&self) -> CircuitStatus;
}
```

## Circuit Breakers to Implement
- browser_auth: For Playwright auth flows
- caut_cli: For account usage queries
- wezterm_cli: For pane operations
- webhook: For notification delivery

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa doctor --circuits

Circuit Breaker Status:
  browser_auth: CLOSED (healthy)
  caut_cli:     OPEN (3 failures, retry in 4m32s)
  wezterm_cli:  CLOSED (healthy)
  webhook:      HALF-OPEN (testing...)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CircuitBreaker struct with state machine
- [ ] Configurable thresholds and cooldowns
- [ ] Status visible in wa doctor
- [ ] Structured logging on state changes

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

# Circuit Breaker Infrastructure

## Purpose
Implement a generic circuit breaker pattern to prevent retry storms when services fail.

## State Machine
```
     failures < threshold
CLOSED ◄────────────────────────► OPEN
  │                                  │
  │ failure >= threshold             │ cooldown elapsed
  └──────────────────►               ▼
                              HALF-OPEN
                                  │
                    success → CLOSED
                    failure → OPEN
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct CircuitBreaker {
    name: String,
    state: CircuitState,
    failure_count: u32,
    failure_threshold: u32,
    cooldown: Duration,
    last_failure: Option<Instant>,
}

impl CircuitBreaker {
    pub fn call<T>(&self, f: impl FnOnce() -> Result<T>) -> Result<T, CircuitError>;
    pub fn record_success(&mut self);
    pub fn record_failure(&mut self, error: &Error);
    pub fn status(&self) -> CircuitStatus;
}
```

## Circuit Breakers to Implement
- browser_auth: For Playwright auth flows
- caut_cli: For account usage queries
- wezterm_cli: For pane operations
- webhook: For notification delivery

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa doctor --circuits

Circuit Breaker Status:
  browser_auth: CLOSED (healthy)
  caut_cli:     OPEN (3 failures, retry in 4m32s)
  wezterm_cli:  CLOSED (healthy)
  webhook:      HALF-OPEN (testing...)
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Configurable thresholds and cooldowns

 {"id":"wa-37x.2","title":"Watchdog/heartbeat system: deadlock detection and auto-recovery","description":"
# Watchdog/Heartbeat System

## Purpose
Monitor wa's own health and recover from hangs or deadlocks.

## How It Works
1. Main loop updates heartbeat timestamp each iteration
2. Watchdog thread checks heartbeat every N seconds
3. If heartbeat stale > threshold, trigger recovery

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct Watchdog {
    heartbeat: Arc<AtomicU64>,  // Unix timestamp
    stale_threshold: Duration,
    recovery_action: RecoveryAction,
}

pub enum RecoveryAction {
    LogWarning,
    DumpDiagnostics,
    ForceRestart,
}
```

## Recovery Sequence
1. Log warning with stack traces
2. Dump diagnostic state (queue depths, lock holders)
3. If still stale after N seconds: force restart
4. Generate crash report with diagnostics

## Diagnostic Dump
When watchdog triggers:
- Thread states
- Queue depths
- Lock holder info (if available)
- Recent event log
- Memory usage

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa status --health

Watchdog:
  Main loop: healthy (last heartbeat 0.1s ago)
  DB writer: healthy (queue: 3 items)
  Ingest: healthy (lag: 15ms)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Heartbeat updated each main loop iteration
- [ ] Watchdog thread monitors heartbeat
- [ ] Recovery actions trigger at configurable threshold
- [ ] Diagnostic dump provides useful context
- [ ] Crash report generated on force restart

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-37x.3","title":"Chaos testing harness: fault injection framework for resilience validation","description":"
# Chaos Testing Harness

## Purpose
Inject failures systematically to validate wa's resilience under adverse conditions.

## Fault Injection Points
```rust
pub trait FaultInjector {
    fn maybe_fail(&self, point: FaultPoint) -> Result<()>;
}

pub enum FaultPoint {
    WezTermCliCall,
    DbWrite,
    DbRead,
    BrowserAction,
    NetworkRequest,
    FileSystemOp,
}
```

## Test Scenarios

### 1. WezTerm Unavailable
- Kill WezTerm during capture
- Verify: wa degrades gracefully, resumes when available

### 2. DB Corruption
- Corrupt SQLite file mid-write
- Verify: wa detects corruption, reports error, doesn't crash

### 3. Network Timeouts
- Inject timeout on webhook calls
- Verify: circuit breaker trips, notifications queue

### 4. Resource Exhaustion
- Fill disk to capacity
- Verify: wa stops writing, logs error, continues observing

### 5. Queue Overflow
- Fill event queue beyond capacity
- Verify: backpressure applied, no data loss

## Harness Design
```rust
pub struct ChaosHarness {
    injectors: HashMap<FaultPoint, Box<dyn FaultInjector>>,
    scenario: ChaosScenario,
}

pub struct ChaosScenario {
    faults: Vec<ScheduledFault>,
    duration: Duration,
    assertions: Vec<ChaosAssertion>,
}
```

## CI Integration
- Chaos tests run in isolated environment
- Failures generate detailed reports
- Key scenarios gated on merge

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fault injection framework implemented
- [ ] At least 5 chaos scenarios defined
- [ ] All scenarios pass (system recovers)
- [ ] Chaos tests run in CI (nightly)

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-37x.4","title":"Retry with exponential backoff: standardized retry policy across all I/O","description":"
# Retry with Exponential Backoff

## Purpose
Provide a consistent, well-tuned retry mechanism for all fallible operations.

## Retry Policy
```rust
pub struct RetryPolicy {
    pub initial_delay: Duration,    // 100ms default
    pub max_delay: Duration,        // 30s default
    pub backoff_factor: f64,        // 2.0 default
    pub jitter_percent: f64,        // 10% default
    pub max_attempts: Option<u32>,  // None = infinite
}

pub async fn with_retry<T>(
    policy: &RetryPolicy,
    operation: impl AsyncFn() -> Result<T>,
) -> Result<T>;
```

## Jitter Formula
```
delay = base_delay * jitter_factor
jitter_factor = 1.0 + random(-jitter_percent, +jitter_percent)
```

## Application Points
- WezTerm CLI calls: 3 attempts, 100ms initial
- DB writes: 5 attempts, 50ms initial
- Webhook delivery: 5 attempts, 1s initial
- Browser actions: 2 attempts, 500ms initial

## Logging
Each retry logged with:
- Attempt number
- Delay applied
- Error that triggered retry

## Integration with Circuit Breaker
- Retries happen within circuit breaker
- Exceeded retries count as circuit failure
- Circuit OPEN prevents retry attempts

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] RetryPolicy struct with configurable parameters
- [ ] with_retry helper function
- [ ] Jitter implemented correctly
- [ ] All I/O points use retry policy
- [ ] Logging shows retry attempts

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.
